title: Executive Order 7856—Rules Governing the Granting and Issuing of Passports in the United States

flags.justification: Sole authority to grant, issue, restrict, withdraw, or cancel a passport rests with the Secretary of State (Part I, para 1; para 124: 'authorized in his discretion to refuse to issue a passport, to restrict a passport for use only in certain countries... to withdraw or cancel a passport already issued'). The phrase 'in his discretion' or equivalent recurs dozens of times with no substantive standard limiting it.

No appeal, review, or reconsideration mechanism is specified anywhere in the text for a denial, restriction, or revocation under para 124-125. The order is silent on any check on the Secretary's exercise of this discretion.
Single coherent subject throughout: procedural rules for passport issuance, amendment, and renewal.

Para 125: a person who 'knowingly use[s] or attempt[s] to use it in violation of the conditions or restrictions... the protection of the United States may be withdrawn from him' — no defined process, standard of proof, or consequence scale for this determination.
No incentive structure identifiable in the text that rewards behavior contrary to the order's purpose. Not an emergency-powers instrument; Zombie Emergency Trap sub-flag does not apply.

No formal sunset, but appropriately so: this is a standing administrative procedures framework, not a grant of emergency or extraordinary authority. The order's own history — it 'supersedes Executive Order No. 5860... as amended by Executive Order No. 6650' (para 127) — demonstrates the rules are revised through ordinary supersession as needed, functioning as a de facto review mechanism.
The text does not reduce or eliminate any existing oversight mechanism (judicial review, IG access, congressional reporting); it is silent on them rather than displacing them.

Reconsidered 2026-07-04 under v1.2: unlike a bare administrative land action, passport administration is an ongoing regulatory relationship (State Department determines applicant eligibility, and para. 124 grants broad discretion to refuse, restrict, or withdraw). The compliance-regime concept does map here. No third party — judicial, congressional, or otherwise — is given standing in the text to check the Secretary's exercise of that discretion. This is a real third-party incentive gap, not a conceptual mismatch; corrected from an original NOT_APPLICABLE mislabel to PRESENT (not merely ABSENT, since the operational test is answered 'no such actor exists').

Para 124's undefined discretionary authority to refuse, restrict, or withdraw passports — combined with no substantive standard — creates latent capacity for the passport mechanism to be used for purposes well beyond routine issuance administration (e.g., foreign-policy travel restriction) without further legislative check. This is a structural feature of the text's breadth, not a claim about intent.
Passport administration is squarely within the State Department's statutory domain (22 U.S.C. §211a cited in the preamble); no conflict with another agency's mandate is created.

Fee exemptions (paras 91-99, for government employees on official business, American seamen, and grave-visiting relatives of deceased servicemembers) are narrow, well-defined, and relieve only the fee — not the underlying citizenship or eligibility requirements. They do not undermine the order's purpose.

notes: Validation batch (Mayer & Price 2002 appendix positive, pre-registration v1.1). First-pass coding; not yet cross-coded for inter-coder reliability.
